passages.explanation: The Pharisees have noticed that Jesus is gaining more followers than John the Baptist — a fact that's attracting unwanted attention. To avoid a confrontation he's not ready for, Jesus leaves Judea and heads north to Galilee. The route takes him through Samaria, a region Jews typically avoided because of centuries of hostility between the two groups. He stops at Jacob's well near Sychar, tired from travelling. It's about noon.

Jesus asks a Samaritan woman for a drink. This breaks multiple social rules at once: Jewish men didn't speak to women in public, and Jews didn't share utensils or interact with Samaritans. The woman immediately calls this out — she's surprised a Jewish man would even acknowledge her, let alone ask her for something.

Jesus shifts the conversation. He tells her that if she knew who she was talking to, she'd be the one asking him for a drink — and he'd give her 'living water.' She takes him literally: you don't even have a bucket and this well is deep. She asks whether he thinks he's greater than Jacob, the patriarch who dug this well. She doesn't yet realise that Jesus is claiming exactly that.

Jesus draws the contrast: water from this well only quenches thirst temporarily, but the water he offers becomes a permanent internal spring leading to eternal life. The woman still takes him literally and asks for this water so she won't have to keep making the trip to the well. The misunderstanding is deliberate on John's part — it highlights the gap between surface-level thinking and what Jesus is actually offering, which is spiritual, not physical.

Jesus abruptly changes the subject and tells her to go get her husband. She says she doesn't have one. Jesus agrees — and then reveals he already knows she's had five husbands and the man she's currently with isn't her husband. He's demonstrating knowledge he couldn't naturally have, which forces her to take him seriously as more than an ordinary traveller.

Recognising Jesus as a prophet, the woman raises a longstanding theological dispute: Samaritans worshipped God on Mount Gerizim, while Jews insisted Jerusalem was the only legitimate place. Jesus says both locations are about to become irrelevant — true worship isn't about geography but about worshipping in spirit and truth. The woman says she knows the Messiah is coming and will explain everything. Jesus responds directly: 'I am he.' This is one of the earliest moments in the gospels where Jesus identifies himself as the Messiah this openly, and he does it to a Samaritan woman — not to the religious establishment.

The disciples return and are visibly surprised to find Jesus talking with a woman, but none of them question him about it. The woman leaves her water jar behind — a small detail suggesting urgency, she's forgotten what she originally came for — and goes into the city to tell people about Jesus. Her testimony is framed as a question rather than a declaration: 'Can this be the Christ?' The townspeople come out to see for themselves.

The disciples try to get Jesus to eat. He says he has food they don't know about — meaning doing God's will sustains him more than physical food. They take him literally, wondering if someone else brought him lunch. Jesus then uses a harvest metaphor: the fields are already ripe for gathering. Other people planted the seeds — the prophets, John the Baptist, the woman who just left — and his disciples will reap the results of that work. The spiritual harvest among the Samaritans is already beginning.

Many Samaritans believe in Jesus because of the woman's testimony. They ask him to stay, and he remains for two days. After hearing him directly, even more believe — and they tell the woman that their faith now rests on their own experience, not just her report. This progression matters: belief moves from secondhand testimony to personal encounter.

Jesus continues to Galilee. John notes that Jesus himself said a prophet has no honour in his own country. The Galileans welcome him, but only because they saw what he did in Jerusalem at the festival — their reception is based on spectacle, not genuine understanding.

Back in Cana, where he turned water into wine, a royal official begs Jesus to heal his dying son in Capernaum. Jesus challenges him: 'Unless you see signs and wonders, you won't believe.' The man persists — he just wants his son to live. Jesus tells him to go home, his son will live. The man believes the word alone, without seeing any proof, and leaves. On the way home his servants confirm the boy recovered at the exact hour Jesus spoke. John identifies this as Jesus' second sign and the contrast with verse 48 is the point: the official believed without seeing, which is the kind of faith Jesus is looking for.
